content.text: Why KPIs Fail Without a Tracking System

Most GenAI cost management efforts stall at the "set a budget" phase. Without a closed-loop system that defines what to measure, baselins current state, sets explicit targets, and triggers corrective action when results drift, budgets become vanity numbers. A FinOps KPI programme turns spend data into a continuous improvement engine.
A KPI without a target is a metric. A target without tracking is a wish. A KPI system closes the loop: measure → compare → act → re-baseline.

The five-phase lifecycle ensures every KPI is grounded in real data (baseline), owned by a named person (define), tested against an honest target, surfaced in time to act (track), and fed back into the next planning cycle (improve). The quarterly re-baseline loop prevents targets from becoming permanently out-of-date as provider pricing and usage patterns shift.
A well-chosen catalogue has 8–12 KPIs. Fewer than six misses important cost signals; more than fifteen creates noise and diffuses ownership. Each KPI must specify:
: short human name plus a stable machine key (e.g.
: an unambiguous expression — no implicit rounding or time-window assumed.
: exact table or API endpoint, including who is responsible for its accuracy.
: one named role (not a team). The owner sets targets and responds to alerts.
: daily, weekly, or monthly. Match the cadence to the decision cycle, not to data availability.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Literal

class Direction(Enum):
    LOWER_IS_BETTER  = "lower"
    HIGHER_IS_BETTER = "higher"

class Cadence(Enum):
    DAILY   = "daily"
    WEEKLY  = "weekly"
    MONTHLY = "monthly"

@dataclass
class KPIDefinition:
    key: str              # e.g. "cost_per_ktoken"
    name: str             # human label
    formula: str          # SQL or expression string for documentation
    data_source: str      # table or endpoint
    owner_role: str
    cadence: Cadence
    direction: Direction
    unit: str             # "$", "%", "ms", etc.

# Canonical catalogue — 10 KPIs covering spend efficiency, budget, quality, optimisation
FINOPS_KPI_CATALOGUE: list[KPIDefinition] = [
    KPIDefinition("cost_per_ktoken",   "Cost per 1k tokens",       "total_spend / tokens * 1000",         "llm_usage_log",   "Platform Eng", Cadence.DAILY,   Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER,  "$"),
    KPIDefinition("cost_per_request",  "Cost per feature request",  "feature_spend / feature_calls",       "feature_cost_log","Product EM",   Cadence.WEEKLY,  Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER,  "$"),
    KPIDefinition("budget_utilisation","Budget utilisation",        "mtd_spend / monthly_budget",          "budget_snapshot", "FinOps Lead",  Cadence.WEEKLY,  Direction.HIGHER_IS_BETTER, "%"),
    KPIDefinition("cache_hit_rate",    "Prompt-cache hit rate",     "cache_tokens / prompt_tokens",        "llm_usage_log",   "Platform Eng", Cadence.DAILY,   Direction.HIGHER_IS_BETTER, "%"),
    KPIDefinition("waste_ratio",       "Idle/waste spend ratio",    "wasted_spend / total_spend",          "waste_audit_log", "FinOps Lead",  Cadence.MONTHLY, Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER,  "%"),
    KPIDefinition("model_concentration","Top-model spend share",    "top_model_spend / total_spend",       "llm_usage_log",   "Platform Eng", Cadence.MONTHLY, Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER,  "%"),
    KPIDefinition("forecast_accuracy", "Forecast vs actual",        "abs(forecast-actual)/actual",         "forecasts",       "FinOps Lead",  Cadence.MONTHLY, Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER,  "%"),
    KPIDefinition("p95_latency_cost",  "P95 cost per latency-ms",  "p95_cost / p95_latency_ms",           "perf_log",        "SRE",          Cadence.WEEKLY,  Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER,  "$"),
    KPIDefinition("ticket_resolution", "FinOps ticket resolution %","closed_in_sla / total_tickets",       "jira_finops",     "FinOps Lead",  Cadence.WEEKLY,  Direction.HIGHER_IS_BETTER, "%"),
    KPIDefinition("optimisation_yield","Optimisation action yield",  "saved_spend / projected_spend",       "action_tracker",  "FinOps Lead",  Cadence.MONTHLY, Direction.HIGHER_IS_BETTER, "%"),
]

KPI_BY_KEY = {k.key: k for k in FINOPS_KPI_CATALOGUE}

A target set without a baseline is arbitrary. The baseline is the distribution of the KPI over the most recent 90 days, excluding known anomaly periods (model outages, price changes, one-off migrations). The correct summary statistic depends on the KPI's use:
Robust to spike days; represents typical load
Plan for above-average months, not average
Pessimistic — improvement target must beat worst quartile
Average error directly measures model quality

import sqlite3, statistics
from datetime import date, timedelta

def compute_baseline(
    conn: sqlite3.Connection,
    kpi_key: str,
    as_of: date,
    lookback_days: int = 90,
    percentile: float = 50.0,  # override per KPI as needed
) -> float:
    """Return the pth-percentile value of kpi_key over the lookback window."""
    start = (as_of - timedelta(days=lookback_days)).isoformat()
    rows = conn.execute(
        "SELECT value FROM kpi_daily_values "
        "WHERE kpi_key = ? AND period_date >= ? AND is_anomaly = 0",
        (kpi_key, start),
    ).fetchall()
    if not rows:
        raise ValueError(f"No data for {kpi_key} in baseline window")
    values = sorted(r[0] for r in rows)
    idx = int(len(values) * percentile / 100)
    return values[min(idx, len(values) - 1)]
Phase 3 — Setting Targets with RAG Thresholds
Each KPI has two targets: a
(the minimum acceptable performance that triggers an escalation if missed) and a
(the aspirational goal that earns recognition). RAG (red/amber/green) thresholds turn these into actionable status signals.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class RAGStatus(Enum):
    GREEN  = "green"   # on or ahead of commit target
    AMBER  = "amber"   # within 10 % of commit (warning zone)
    RED    = "red"     # worse than commit

@dataclass
class KPITarget:
    kpi_key: str
    quarter: str            # e.g. "2026-Q3"
    baseline_value: float
    commit_target: float    # must-hit by end of quarter
    stretch_target: float   # aspirational
    owner_role: str

    def rag_status(self, current_value: float) -> RAGStatus:
        kpi = KPI_BY_KEY[self.kpi_key]
        if kpi.direction == Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER:
            if current_value <= self.commit_target:
                return RAGStatus.GREEN
            if current_value <= self.commit_target * 1.10:
                return RAGStatus.AMBER
            return RAGStatus.RED
        else:  # HIGHER_IS_BETTER
            if current_value >= self.commit_target:
                return RAGStatus.GREEN
            if current_value >= self.commit_target * 0.90:
                return RAGStatus.AMBER
            return RAGStatus.RED

def set_quarterly_targets(
    baseline_by_key: dict[str, float],
    improvement_pct: float = 0.10,   # 10 % improvement commit
    stretch_pct: float = 0.20,       # 20 % stretch
) -> list[KPITarget]:
    """Auto-derive targets from baselines; refine manually before publishing."""
    targets = []
    for kpi in FINOPS_KPI_CATALOGUE:
        b = baseline_by_key[kpi.key]
        if kpi.direction == Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER:
            commit  = b * (1 - improvement_pct)
            stretch = b * (1 - stretch_pct)
        else:
            commit  = b * (1 + improvement_pct)
            stretch = b * (1 + stretch_pct)
        targets.append(KPITarget(
            kpi_key        = kpi.key,
            quarter        = "2026-Q3",
            baseline_value = b,
            commit_target  = round(commit, 6),
            stretch_target = round(stretch, 6),
            owner_role     = kpi.owner_role,
        ))
    return targets
Once targets are set, a weekly automated job computes each KPI from the source system, compares it against its commit target, and emits a RAG status. The job persists results to a
table that powers dashboards and trend charts.

import sqlite3
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class KPISnapshot:
    kpi_key: str
    week_ending: date
    current_value: float
    baseline_value: float
    commit_target: float
    stretch_target: float
    rag: str               # "green" | "amber" | "red"
    delta_vs_target: float # positive = improvement (regardless of direction)

def compute_weekly_snapshot(
    conn: sqlite3.Connection,
    target: KPITarget,
    week_ending: date,
) -> KPISnapshot:
    current = conn.execute(
        "SELECT value FROM kpi_daily_values "
        "WHERE kpi_key = ? AND period_date <= ? ORDER BY period_date DESC LIMIT 1",
        (target.kpi_key, week_ending.isoformat()),
    ).fetchone()
    if not current:
        raise ValueError(f"No current data for {target.kpi_key}")
    val = current[0]
    rag = target.rag_status(val).value
    kpi = KPI_BY_KEY[target.kpi_key]
    if kpi.direction == Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER:
        delta = target.commit_target - val   # positive = we're below target (good)
    else:
        delta = val - target.commit_target   # positive = we're above target (good)
    return KPISnapshot(
        kpi_key         = target.kpi_key,
        week_ending     = week_ending,
        current_value   = val,
        baseline_value  = target.baseline_value,
        commit_target   = target.commit_target,
        stretch_target  = target.stretch_target,
        rag             = rag,
        delta_vs_target = round(delta, 6),
    )

def persist_snapshot(conn: sqlite3.Connection, snap: KPISnapshot) -> None:
    conn.execute(
        "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO kpi_weekly_snapshot "
        "(kpi_key, week_ending, current_value, baseline_value, commit_target, "
        " stretch_target, rag, delta_vs_target) VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)",
        (snap.kpi_key, snap.week_ending.isoformat(), snap.current_value,
         snap.baseline_value, snap.commit_target, snap.stretch_target,
         snap.rag, snap.delta_vs_target),
    )
    conn.commit()
Phase 5 — Improvement Actions When KPIs Are Red
A red KPI triggers a structured improvement workflow, not an ad-hoc investigation. The workflow has three time-boxed phases:
: identify whether the regression is a data quality issue, a one-time event, or a genuine trend. Only genuine trends proceed to root-cause analysis.
: decompose the KPI into its numerator and denominator, then into cost dimensions (model, team, feature, call pattern). Use the FinOps playbook for the suspected cause category.
: implement one targeted fix, set a re-check date, and measure impact. Avoid broad multi-variable interventions — they make causality unverifiable.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date, timedelta

class TriageOutcome(Enum):
    DATA_QUALITY   = "data_quality"   # pipeline issue; fix source, re-compute
    ONE_TIME_EVENT = "one_time_event" # mark as anomaly; exclude from baseline
    GENUINE_TREND  = "genuine_trend"  # proceed to root-cause

class RootCauseCategory(Enum):
    MODEL_UPGRADE      = "model_upgrade"
    TRAFFIC_SPIKE      = "traffic_spike"
    PROMPT_REGRESSION  = "prompt_regression"
    CACHE_DEGRADATION  = "cache_degradation"
    NEW_FEATURE        = "new_feature"
    VENDOR_PRICE_CHANGE= "vendor_price_change"
    UNKNOWN            = "unknown"

@dataclass
class ImprovementAction:
    kpi_key: str
    created_date: date
    triage_outcome: TriageOutcome
    root_cause: RootCauseCategory
    action_description: str
    owner: str
    due_date: date
    recheck_date: date
    resolved: bool = False
    measured_improvement: float | None = None   # post-action delta

def create_improvement_action(
    snap: KPISnapshot,
    root_cause: RootCauseCategory,
    action_description: str,
    owner: str,
    resolution_days: int = 14,
) -> ImprovementAction:
    today = date.today()
    return ImprovementAction(
        kpi_key              = snap.kpi_key,
        created_date         = today,
        triage_outcome       = TriageOutcome.GENUINE_TREND,
        root_cause           = root_cause,
        action_description   = action_description,
        owner                = owner,
        due_date             = today + timedelta(days=resolution_days),
        recheck_date         = today + timedelta(days=resolution_days + 7),
    )

def measure_action_impact(
    action: ImprovementAction,
    before_value: float,
    after_value: float,
) -> float:
    """Return signed delta: positive means the KPI moved in the desired direction."""
    kpi = KPI_BY_KEY[action.kpi_key]
    if kpi.direction == Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER:
        delta = before_value - after_value   # reduction is positive
    else:
        delta = after_value - before_value   # increase is positive
    action.measured_improvement = delta
    action.resolved = True
    return delta

At the end of each quarter, all KPIs are re-baselined on the most recent 90 days. If a KPI's new baseline is better than the previous commit target, the next quarter's commit target must be set at or above the new baseline — you cannot regress to worse performance after demonstrating an improvement.

def rebaseline_for_next_quarter(
    conn: sqlite3.Connection,
    current_targets: list[KPITarget],
    next_quarter: str,
    as_of: date,
) -> list[KPITarget]:
    """
    Compute new baselines. Enforce a ratchet: commit target >= previous commit.
    """
    new_targets = []
    for t in current_targets:
        new_baseline = compute_baseline(conn, t.kpi_key, as_of)
        kpi = KPI_BY_KEY[t.kpi_key]
        # Improvement direction determines the ratchet comparison
        if kpi.direction == Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER:
            # baseline improved (lower) → use it; otherwise keep old commit
            new_commit = min(new_baseline * 0.90, t.commit_target)
        else:
            new_commit = max(new_baseline * 1.10, t.commit_target)
        new_targets.append(KPITarget(
            kpi_key        = t.kpi_key,
            quarter        = next_quarter,
            baseline_value = new_baseline,
            commit_target  = round(new_commit, 6),
            stretch_target = round(new_commit * (0.80 if kpi.direction == Direction.LOWER_IS_BETTER else 1.20), 6),
            owner_role     = t.owner_role,
        ))
    return new_targets
: tracking total spend growth without normalising by usage volume — a rising total is expected as the product scales.
: assigning a KPI to a team rather than a named role. When everyone owns it, nobody investigates regressions.
: a catalogue with 25+ KPIs dilutes attention. Owners stop checking because the dashboard feels overwhelming.
: if a red KPI triggers no action and no conversation, teams learn targets are decorative.
: re-running the same targets for 12 months after the pricing environment has changed dramatically, making green = mediocre.
: treating pipeline outages as genuine KPI regressions. Always confirm the source data is intact before escalating.
Integration With the Broader FinOps System
KPI tracking does not stand alone — it connects to the other components of the FinOps programme:
Rate card review → check provider price changes or model tier drift
Prompt engineering review → analyse cache key structure
Forecast recalculation → mid-month alert to budget owner
Idle resource audit → license rightsizing review
Forecasting model retrain → review input feature lag
Action quality review → playbook updates for current pattern

A FinOps KPI programme for GenAI spend is a five-phase loop: define a catalogue of 8–12 KPIs with named owners and unambiguous formulas; establish 90-day baselines using appropriate percentiles; set quarterly commit and stretch targets with RAG thresholds; run a weekly automated tracking job; and trigger structured improvement workflows for red KPIs. The quarterly re-baseline ratchet ensures targets tighten over time rather than drifting into meaninglessness. The hardest discipline is cultural: KPIs must trigger real consequences — conversations, actions, and measured follow-up — to produce improvement rather than just reporting.
